title: Improving CFT Operators Using Machine Learning
authors: Lior Oppenheim, Snir Gazit, Zohar Ringel
abstract: Finite-size effects limit the accuracy with which conformal data can be extracted from lattice simulations of critical systems. While action improvement suppresses some corrections to scaling, it does not address operator-dependent effects arising from imperfect lattice representations of continuum conformal fields. In this work, we propose a data-driven method for improving lattice operators themselves, constructing estimators with enhanced overlap with the corresponding primary operators of the continuum conformal field theory. We identify improved lattice representations of leading spin and energy operators in three two-dimensional critical systems: the Ising model, the q = 3 Potts model, and the dilute q = 3 Potts model. In all cases, the resulting operators exhibit reduced corrections to scaling and yield more accurate estimates of scaling dimensions compared to conventional lattice choices. The code and analysis workflows used to produce these results are made available in an accompanying GitHub repository.
summary: This work introduces a machine learning technique, RSMI-NE, to construct improved lattice operators for critical quantum systems. By training neural networks to maximize mutual information, the method significantly reduces systematic errors, particularly analytic corrections, when extracting fundamental CFT scaling dimensions. This demonstrates a powerful synergy between information theory and critical phenomena analysis.
main_problem: The paper addresses the systematic error in extracting continuum Conformal Field Theory (CFT) data from lattice simulations due to imperfect lattice representations of continuum operators.
main_result: A data-driven, machine-learning approach successfully constructs lattice operators that significantly reduce analytic corrections to scaling compared to conventional lattice choices across multiple critical models.
method: The authors employ a Real-Space Mutual Information Neural Estimator (RSMI-NE) to train neural networks that map local lattice configurations to operators, maximizing mutual information with the environment.
model_or_system: The study is applied to three two-dimensional critical systems on a square lattice: the Ising model, the q=3 Potts model, and the dilute q=3 Potts model.
key_observables: Scaling dimensions ($\Delta_O$) of leading spin ($\sigma$) and energy ($\epsilon$) operators, and the ratio of connected two-point functions ($	ext{R}_{	ext{O}}(r)$).
important_parameters: Finite size $L$, correlation distance $r$, and the coupling constants defining the critical points.
main_assumptions: The existence of a CFT description for the critical point, and the assumption that the optimal lattice operator can be learned via maximizing mutual information.
figures_summary: Figures compare finite-size scaling of two-point correlation functions for spin and energy operators, contrasting naive vs. neural estimators, and visualizing the structure of learned polynomial surrogates.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the theoretical problem of lattice corrections to CFT scaling. It then details the RSMI-NE methodology, training the network to maximize mutual information. Finally, it applies this method to the Ising and Potts models, presenting quantitative comparisons of scaling dimension estimates and correlation functions.
why_interesting: The use of information theory and neural networks to systematically improve physical observables extracted from lattice simulations provides a powerful, data-driven tool applicable to complex many-body systems where traditional renormalization group methods struggle with finite-size corrections.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Finite-size scaling of two-point correlation functions using Sandvik’s method (see eq. 2). The six panels show the scaling for the leading spin (σ) and energy (ϵ) operators in the 2d Ising, q = 3 Potts, and dilute q = 3 Potts models, comparing naive lattice operators with machine-learned (neural) estimators. For the Ising and dilute Potts models, the data are fitted to a1L−2 + a2L−4 + ∆O, reflecting purely analytic corrections to scaling. For the q = 3 Potts model, the fit includes both non-analytic and analytic terms, a1L−0.8 + a2L−1.6 + a3L−2 + ∆O. The coefficients of the non-analytic corrections (L−0.8 and L−1.6) are constrained to be identical for the naive and neural operators,...

FIG. 3. Low-order structure of the fitted surrogates within the spin field. (A) Linear filter for the Z2-odd spin operator σ, shown as site weights. (B) Quadratic surrogate for the Z2- even energy operator ϵ, visualized as a weighted graph over pairs of sites (edge color encodes sign and magnitude). (C) A representative spin configuration that maximizes the cu- bic features of the σ surrogate; the minimizing configuration follows from a global Z2 flip. Spin transparency encodes lo- cal sensitivity: more opaque sites are those whose flip would produce a larger decrease in the cubic component (i.e., they contribute more strongly to the maximum).

FIG. 2. Scaling performance of low-order surrogates for the learned Ising operators. (A) Finite-size scaling analysis for the spin operator σ, comparing the naive lattice estimator, the neural estimator, and polynomial surrogates obtained by fit- ting the neural operator to linear and linear plus cubic forms. The linear surrogate’s scaling resembles that of the naive es- timator, indicating that cubic terms are required to repro- duce the neural operator’s improved scaling behavior. (B) Corresponding analysis for the energy operator ϵ, comparing the naive estimator, the neural estimator, and a quadratic surrogate. The quadratic surrogate closely matches the neu- ral estimator, indicating...

FIG. 4. Ratio RO(r) of connected two-point functions for the neural and naive operators in the Ising model at L = 24. The enhancement is substantial for the energy operator ϵ and modest for the spin operator σ, consistent with the larger reduction of finite-size corrections observed for ϵ in the main text.
